title: practical english usage - Part 13
content:
past progressive (or continuous)  a verb tense made with was/were + .. .ing.

(with had been + .. .ing) are past perfect progressive (or continuous).

raining. The  first two examples are simple past perfect; the last two forgotten; The children had arrived; She had been working; It had been past perfect  a verb tense made with had + past participle. Examples: I had necessarily past, in spite of the name.) form perfect tenses and passives, or as an adjective. (The meaning is not past participle  a verb form like broken, gone, stopped, which  can be used to (passive). See also active.

prison for five years (active) and Lucas was sent to prison for five years thing that is affected by the action of the verb. Compare: They sent Lucas to active verb forms). The  subject of a passive verb form is usually the person or is broken; was told; will be helped (but not breaks, told, will help, which  are passive  A  passive verb form is made with be + past participle. Examples: a couple of hours to spare, I went to see a film.

tense. Examples: Discouraged by his failure, he resigned from his job; Having participle clause  a clause-like structure which  contains a participle, not a verb participle see present participle and past participle.

See also subject.

gave me a watch, the indirect object is me, and the direct object is a watch.

a person (or people) who  rec eive(s) the direct object. In the sentence Anna dog for a walk, the dog is the direct object. The  indirect object usually refers to people or things) affected by the action of the verb. In the sentence Take the active clause. The  direct object most often refers to a person or thing (or object  a noun phrase or pronoun that normally comes after the verb in an this and these are differences of number.

grammatically. The  differences between house and houses, mouse and mice, number  the way in which  differences between singular and plural are shown the subject, object or complement in a clause. Example: the last bus.

noun phrase  a group of words (e.g. article + adjective + noun) which  acts as mostly used without articles.

and place names (e.g. Birmingham) are called 'proper nouns'; they are are most often the names of people or things. Personal names (e.g. George) noun  a word like oil, memory, arm, which  can be used with an article. Nouns Language terminology •  xxvi Examples: to be going; We were wondering; I'll be seeing you.

progressive (or continuous)  A  verb form made with the auxiliary be +.. .ing.

present simple  see simple present.

Examples: I am going; She is staying for two weeks.

present progressive (or continuous)  a verb tense made with  am/are/is + .. .ing.

two (with have been +.. .ing) are present perfect progressive (or present perfect It has been raining. The  first two examples are simple present perfect; the last I have forgotten; The children have arrived; I've been working all day, present perfect  a verb tense made with have/has + past participle. Examples: in spite of the name.) See also gerund.

he started to read; She was running. (The meaning is not necessarily present, a verb or part of a verb. Examples: a crying baby; Opening his newspaper, present participle  the form of a verb ending in -ing, used as an adjective, verb form + preposition. Examples: insist on; care for; listen to.

prepositional verb  a verb form that is made up of two parts: pronoun.

preposition  a word like on, off, of, into, normally followed by a noun or somebody at the door.

preparatory subject in there is... and similar structures. Example: There is Example: He made it clear that he disagreed. There is used as a kind of enough sleep, It can also be used as a preparatory object in certain structures.

use the pronoun it as a preparatory subject. Example: It is important to get infinitive or a clause, we  usually put it towards the end of the sentence and preparatory subject, preparatory object  When  the subject of a sentence is an in an invited audience. See also postmodifier.

premodifier  a word that comes before the word which  it modifies, e.g. invited unhappy. See also suffix.
